rulings.Case Title: RONALD D. CARTER VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER to Amended PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 16, 2026, Line 1, RESPONDENT STATE OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER to Amended PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION. Respondent State of California's demurrer to the second amended petition for a writ of mandate filed by petitioner Ronald Carter is sustained without leave to amend. The demurrer is sustained because, despite being given several opportunities to allege a viable writ claim against the State, Mr. Carter has not alleged that the State has a mandatory duty to investigate the theft of his food benefits and/or to restore those benefits to him. The demurrer is sustained without leave to amend because Mr. Carter's own filings show that he is unaware of any such mandatory duty imposed on the State, although he believes that both good policy and fairness supports the imposition of such a duty. While the court is sympathetic to the plight suffered by Mr. Carter from the theft of his governmental food benefits, the court lacks the authority to require the State of California to restore those benefits to Mr. Carter in the absence of a mandatory duty imposed on the State to do so. Mr. Carter is mistaken in his interpretation of Judge Van Aken's November 19, 2025 order. That order did not determine that Mr. Carter had sufficiently alleged a mandatory duty that the State had not complied with. The order did not address the mandatory duty issue, thereby leaving the issue open for adjudication on the State's demurrer to Mr. Carter's second amended petition. Because Mr. Carter's failure to allege a mandatory duty by the State requires, without more, the sustaining the State's demurrer without leave to amend, this order does not address the issue of whether Mr. Carter sufficiently alleged compliance with the Government Claims Act. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/HEK).

rulings.Case Title: ASHLEY BURNSTAD VS. VOLKSWAGEN GROUP OF AMERICA, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Award Of Attorneys Fees, Costs, And Expenses
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 16, 2026, Line 4, PLAINTIFF ASHLEY BURNSTAD's Motion For Award Of Attorneys Fees, Costs, And Expenses. Plaintiff Ashley Burnstad's motion for attorneys' fees and expenses is granted in part. Burnstad is awarded reasonable attorneys' fees of $18,000 and expenses of $710.55 for a total of 18,710.55. Based on the court file and the court's extensive experience with lemon law and other repetitive litigation in San Francisco, the court created its own lodestar of 45 hours of attorney work at a blended hourly rate of $400 per hour and no compensable paralegal work. This lemon law case presented no novel issues and involved relatively minimal litigation activity. Some of the billing entries of plaintiff's attorneys concerned work not compensable under the applicable statute (see, e.g. 5/8/25 and 5/29/25 entries re possible UCL claim) and some of the entries were excessive for the time claimed (see, e.g., 8/31/25 entry of an hour for research on objecting to a 998 offer, the 10/24/25 entry re research on a second 998 offer, the entries re drafting the fees motion and the 6.1 hours claimed for the reply papers on this motion). While the court does not doubt that the attorneys spent the time they claimed, only reasonably spent time is compensable, not what the attorneys choose to spend. In San Francisco the prevailing hourly rates for attorneys engaged in repetitive litigation are significantly lower than the rates sought by plaintiff, especially where, as here, much of the attorney work was performed by an attorney who had been admitted to the bar less than two years ago. The entries for minimal paralegal time claimed by plaintiff are more consistent with overhead/clerical work than work that is separately billed by a law office. There is no basis for either a positive or negative multiplier. Volkswagen does not dispute the claimed expenses. rulings.Case Title: BRETT LAMY VS. PAUL DIMAURO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 16, 2026, Line 5, DEFENDANT PAUL DIMAURO's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS. Defendant Paul DiMauro's motion to quash service of summons and to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction and forum non conveniens is denied in its entirety. Mr. DiMauro's solicitation of a California resident to participate in his investments in securities constitutes "purposeful availment of forum benefits" and thus is sufficient to impose specific personal jurisdiction over him in a case where plaintiff's claim is based on his participation in those investments. This is true even though Mr. DiMauro's contacts were only via text messages. Zehia v. Superior Court (2020) 45 Cal. App. 5th 543, Moncrief v. Clark (2015) 238 Cal. App. 4th 1000, and Hall v. LaRonde (1997) 56 Cal. App. 4th 1342 support specific jurisdiction over Mr. DiMauro. Nor has Mr. DiMauro presented a "compelling case" that requiring him to defend this case in California is unfair or unreasonable. (Moncrief, 238 Cal. App. 4th at 1008). Mr. DiMauro has not satisfied his burden of showing that this court is an inconvenient forum. While Mr. Lamy no longer resides in California, his choice of a California forum is entitled to "due deference." (National Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal. App. 4th 902, 929-930). Notably Mr. DiMauro does not address the balance of private and public interest factors , which is part of the burden he bears on this motion. (Morris v. AGFA Corp. (2006) 144 Cal. App. 4th 1452, 1463). Given the partial overlap of this case with the San Francisco venued marital dissolution case between Mr. Lamy and Mr. DiMauro's daughter, there is a significant public interest for this case to remain in this court. (See, e.g., Klein v. Superior Court (1988) 198 Cal. App. 3d 894, 901 (significant weight given to location of related litigation)). Moreover, relatively speaking, this is a simple case that would have at most a de minimis impact on court congestion. (Roulier v. Cannondale (2002) 101 Cal. App. 4th 1180. 1189-1190). rulings.Case Title: KIMBERLY MAYS VS. SUTTER HEALTH - SUTTER BAY HOSPITALS, DBA ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 16, 2026, Line 6, DEFENDANT ACTALENT SCIENTIFIC LLC's DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT. (Complete tentative ruling emailed to the parties). Defendant Actalent Scientific LLC's demurrer to all seven causes of action alleged against it in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Kimberly Mays is sustained with leave to amend as to the second cause of action for hostile work environment, the third cause of action for failure to prevent, and the eighth cause of action for constructive discharge and sustained without leave to amend as to the fourth cause of action for negligent hiring, retention and/or supervision, the fifth cause of action for sexual assault and battery, the sixth cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress, and the seventh cause of action for negligent infliction of emotional distress.
